[ACCU](/wiki/Association_of_Catholic_Colleges_and_Universities "Association of Catholic Colleges and Universities"), [AJCU](/wiki/Association_of_Jesuit_Colleges_and_Universities "Association of Jesuit Colleges and Universities")

(**Loyola** [/lɔɪˈoʊlə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")[[4]](#cite_note-4) or **LUC**) is a [private](/wiki/Private_university "Private university") [Jesuit](/wiki/Society_of_Jesus "Society of Jesus") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") in [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"), Illinois, United States. Founded in 1870 by the Society of Jesus, Loyola is one of the largest [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") universities in the United States. Its namesake is Saint [Ignatius of Loyola](/wiki/Ignatius_of_Loyola "Ignatius of Loyola"). Loyola's professional schools include programs in medicine, nursing, and health sciences anchored by the [Loyola University Medical Center](/wiki/Loyola_University_Medical_Center "Loyola University Medical Center"),[[5]](#cite_note-5) and the [Loyola University Chicago School of Law](/wiki/Loyola_University_Chicago_School_of_Law "Loyola University Chicago School of Law"). It is [classified](/wiki/Carnegie_Classification_of_Institutions_of_Higher_Education "Carnegie Classification of Institutions of Higher Education") among "R1: Doctoral Universities – Very high research spending and doctorate production".[[6]](#cite_note-6)

**Loyola University Chicago** (**Loyola** [/lɔɪˈoʊlə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")[[4]](#cite_note-4) or **LUC**) is a [private](/wiki/Private_university "Private university") [Jesuit](/wiki/Society_of_Jesus "Society of Jesus") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") in [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"), Illinois, United States. Founded in 1870 by the Society of Jesus, Loyola is one of the largest [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") universities in the United States. Its namesake is Saint [Ignatius of Loyola](/wiki/Ignatius_of_Loyola "Ignatius of Loyola"). Loyola's professional schools include programs in medicine, nursing, and health sciences anchored by the [Loyola University Medical Center](/wiki/Loyola_University_Medical_Center "Loyola University Medical Center"),[[5]](#cite_note-5) and the [Loyola University Chicago School of Law](/wiki/Loyola_University_Chicago_School_of_Law "Loyola University Chicago School of Law"). It is [classified](/wiki/Carnegie_Classification_of_Institutions_of_Higher_Education "Carnegie Classification of Institutions of Higher Education") among "R1: Doctoral Universities – Very high research spending and doctorate production".[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Comprising thirteen colleges and schools, Loyola offers more than 80 undergraduate and 140 graduate/professional programs and enrolls approximately 17,000 students.[[7]](#cite_note-state2015-7) Loyola has five campuses across the [Chicago metropolitan area](/wiki/Chicago_metropolitan_area "Chicago metropolitan area"), as well as a campus in [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"). Another guest program in [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing") was closed in 2018.[[8]](#cite_note-8) The flagship Lake Shore Campus is on the shores of [Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan") in the [Rogers Park](/wiki/Rogers_Park,_Chicago "Rogers Park, Chicago") and [Edgewater](/wiki/Edgewater,_Chicago "Edgewater, Chicago") neighborhoods of Chicago, just over seven miles (11.3 km) north of [the Loop](/wiki/Chicago_Loop "Chicago Loop").

Loyola's athletic teams, nicknamed the [Ramblers](/wiki/Loyola_Ramblers "Loyola Ramblers"), compete in [NCAA Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") as members of the [Atlantic 10 Conference](/wiki/Atlantic_10_Conference "Atlantic 10 Conference").[[9]](#cite_note-9) Loyola won the 1963 NCAA men's basketball championship and remains the only school from Illinois to do so.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The Ramblers are also two-time (2014, 2015) NCAA champions in men's volleyball.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Loyola_University_Chicago&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Madonna_della_Strada_Chapel.JPG/250px-Madonna_della_Strada_Chapel.JPG)](/wiki/File:Madonna_della_Strada_Chapel.JPG)

[Madonna Della Strada Chapel](/wiki/Madonna_Della_Strada_Chapel "Madonna Della Strada Chapel")

On June 30, 1870, Jesuit priest and educator [Arnold Damen](/wiki/Arnold_Damen "Arnold Damen") established **St. Ignatius College**.[[12]](#cite_note-12) At that time, Chicago was a much smaller, but rapidly growing city just shy of 300,000 people, and as a result, the original campus was much closer to the city center, along Roosevelt Road. In 1909, the school was renamed **Loyola University**, and in 1912, it began to move to the Lake Shore Campus; today the original building is part of [St. Ignatius College Prep](/wiki/St._Ignatius_College_Prep "St. Ignatius College Prep"), adjacent to the [University of Illinois at Chicago](/wiki/University_of_Illinois_at_Chicago "University of Illinois at Chicago").

To meet the growing needs of Chicago, Loyola established professional schools in law (1908), medicine (1909), business (1922), and nursing (1935). The Chicago College of Dental Surgery became part of the university in 1923, and closed 70 years later. A downtown campus was founded in 1914, and with it, the School of Sociology. As the predecessor to the School of Social Work, it enrolled Loyola's first female students, though the school did not become fully coeducational until 1966. [Loyola Academy](/wiki/Loyola_Academy "Loyola Academy"), a college prep high school, occupied Dumbach Hall on the Lake Shore Campus, until it moved to [Wilmette](/wiki/Wilmette,_Illinois "Wilmette, Illinois") in 1957.

The current Water Tower Campus opened in 1949. In 1962, Loyola opened a campus in Rome, near the site of the [1960 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1960_Summer_Olympics "1960 Summer Olympics"). In 1969, Loyola established the School of Education and consolidated medical programs at the Loyola University Medical Center, a hospital and health care complex in [Maywood](/wiki/Maywood,_Illinois "Maywood, Illinois"), a neighboring suburb of Chicago. The university legally separated from the Jesuits in 1970, and today is under lay control and governed by a board of trustees. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Loyola purchased neighboring [Mundelein College](/wiki/Mundelein_College "Mundelein College") in 1991.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Mundelin_Center%2C_Loyola_University_Chicago.JPG/250px-Mundelin_Center%2C_Loyola_University_Chicago.JPG)](/wiki/File:Mundelin_Center,_Loyola_University_Chicago.JPG)

Mundelein Center

The university faced severe financial hardships and dwindling enrollment during the late 1990s.[[13]](#cite_note-:0-13) [Michael J. Garanzini](/wiki/Michael_J._Garanzini "Michael J. Garanzini") S.J. was instated as president of Loyola in 2001 and led major capital campaigns to improve Loyola's academic profile and campuses.[[13]](#cite_note-:0-13)[[14]](#cite_note-:1-14) In 2005, the [Loyola University Museum of Art](/wiki/Loyola_University_Museum_of_Art "Loyola University Museum of Art") was established on the Water Tower Campus, and the Rome campus was renamed in honor of Director Emeritus [John P. Felice](/wiki/John_Felice_Rome_Center "John Felice Rome Center"). In 2009, the Cuneo Foundation presented the university with the [Cuneo Mansion and Gardens](/wiki/Cuneo_Museum "Cuneo Museum"),[[15]](#cite_note-15) a 100-acre (40 ha) estate with an Italianate mansion and extensive collections of art and furnishings in [Vernon Hills](/wiki/Vernon_Hills,_Illinois "Vernon Hills, Illinois"). The $50 million gift was one of the largest in Loyola history.

In 2010, Loyola purchased the Resurrection Retreat Center in [Woodstock](/wiki/Woodstock,_Illinois "Woodstock, Illinois") for retreats and ecological study, allowing the site to become the school's fifth campus. In 2012, Loyola alumnus [Michael R. Quinlan](/wiki/Michael_R._Quinlan "Michael R. Quinlan") donated $40 million to the business school, which was renamed in his honor. During this time, over 200,000 square feet of LEED-certified sustainable spaces have been built on the Lake Shore Campus, along with significant mixed-use developments on the Water Tower Campus.

In 2015, the university established Arrupe College, a uniquely structured two-year college designed to give low-income students access to a Loyola education.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

On May 23, 2016, Loyola named [Jo Ann Rooney](/wiki/Jo_Ann_Rooney "Jo Ann Rooney") its 24th president. She was the school's first female president. She served until the end of September 2022 when [Mark Reed](/wiki/Mark_Reed_(academic) "Mark Reed (academic)") succeeded her.

## Campuses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Loyola_University_Chicago&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Campuses")]

### Lake Shore Campus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Loyola_University_Chicago&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Lake Shore Campus")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Cudahy_Library.2.JPG/250px-Cudahy_Library.2.JPG)](/wiki/File:Cudahy_Library.2.JPG)

Cudahy Library

Loyola's flagship Lake Shore Campus is on the shores of Lake Michigan in the Rogers Park and Edgewater neighborhoods on Chicago's north side, about seven and a half miles north of the Loop.[[17]](#cite_note-17) Founded in 1912, it is the school's primary residential campus and the home of the College of Arts and Sciences and a variety of graduate programs. With over 40 buildings, the campus offers green space and lakeshore access, as well as several landmarks:
The [Madonna della Strada Chapel](/wiki/Madonna_Della_Strada_Chapel "Madonna Della Strada Chapel"), an Art Deco masterpiece completed in 1939, is the center of Loyola's religious life. The [Mundelein Center](/wiki/Mundelein_College "Mundelein College"), a 200-foot tall Art Deco skyscraper completed in 1930, is the home of Loyola's fine and performing arts programs and a [National Historical Landmark](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark").[[18]](#cite_note-18) The [Joseph J. Gentile Arena](/wiki/Joseph_J._Gentile_Arena "Joseph J. Gentile Arena"), which holds 5,500 for basketball, volleyball, and campus events, was recently expanded to include the Norville Center, a student-athlete academic center and home of Rambler athletics. One of the largest events held annually in Gentile Arena is Colossus, which features a musical artist and comedian. Artists, including [Jason Derulo](/wiki/Jason_Derulo "Jason Derulo") and [John Mulaney](/wiki/John_Mulaney "John Mulaney"), have performed for Colossus. The Halas Recreation Center was remodeled and incorporated into the sprawling new Damen Student Center, the heart of campus social life. Mertz Hall is considered a hub for friendship and amongst the best of the resident halls.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/Cudahy_Science_Hall.JPG/250px-Cudahy_Science_Hall.JPG)](/wiki/File:Cudahy_Science_Hall.JPG)

Cudahy Science Hall

The E.M. Cudahy Memorial Library contains over 900,000 volumes and 3,600 periodical subscriptions,[[19]](#cite_note-:2-19) and in 2008, was expanded to include the Information Commons, an academic and social space with glass curtain walls that offer unparalleled views of the lake and campus. Science education and research takes place in the Quinlan Life Sciences building and the Institute of Environmental Sustainability, a multipurpose complex that includes academic space, a residence hall, a greenhouse, and the largest geothermal energy facility in Chicago.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

#### Sustainability

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Loyola_University_Chicago&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Sustainability")]

Loyola's various environmental efforts have reduced university energy use by 33% since 1998.[[21]](#cite_note-21) Loyola has three [LEED](/wiki/LEED_certified "LEED certified") Silver certified buildings and four LEED Gold certified buildings, with all future construction to be LEED certified as well. Loyola has more green roofs than any college in the Midwest, which includes both new and renovated buildings.

### Water Tower Campus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Loyola_University_Chicago&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Water Tower Campus")]

The Water Tower Campus opened in 1949, along a stretch of [Michigan Avenue](/wiki/Michigan_Avenue_(Chicago) "Michigan Avenue (Chicago)") today known as the *[Magnificent Mile](/wiki/Magnificent_Mile "Magnificent Mile")*, and is named after the [Chicago Water Tower](/wiki/Chicago_Water_Tower "Chicago Water Tower"), a city landmark that survived the [Great Chicago Fire](/wiki/Great_Chicago_Fire "Great Chicago Fire") of 1871. It is the home of the Quinlan School of Business, the School of Law, the School of Education, the School of Continuing and Professional Studies, the School of Social Work, the School of Communication, the Institute of Pastoral Studies, and Arrupe College, as well as a selection of classes from programs based elsewhere.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Water_Tower_Chicago_2012-0261.jpg/250px-Water_Tower_Chicago_2012-0261.jpg)](/wiki/File:Water_Tower_Chicago_2012-0261.jpg)

The Chicago Water Tower, a city landmark and the downtown campus' namesake

Dominated by mixed-use skyscrapers, campus buildings include the Corboy Law Center, Terry Student Center, Baumhart Hall, and landmark Lewis Towers, built in 1945 as the Illinois Catholic Women's Club,[[22]](#cite_note-22) and today home of the Office of the President and the Loyola University Museum of Art. Recent construction includes the School of Communication which occupies the basement, first, and second floors of [The Clare](/wiki/The_Clare "The Clare"), a 587-foot tower that serves as a retirement community, and the LEED-certified John and Kathy Schreiber Center, which opened in 2015 as the new home of the Quinlan School of Business.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

The campus is steps from the offices of major corporations, Chicago's premiere retail and tourist district, as well as institutions like the [Museum of Contemporary Art](/wiki/Museum_of_Contemporary_Art,_Chicago "Museum of Contemporary Art, Chicago") and the [Newberry Library](/wiki/Newberry_Library "Newberry Library"). [Holy Name Cathedral](/wiki/Holy_Name_Cathedral,_Chicago "Holy Name Cathedral, Chicago"), the seat of the [Archdiocese of Chicago](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Chicago "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Chicago"), is directly adjacent to the south.

### Health Sciences Campus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Loyola_University_Chicago&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Health Sciences Campus")]

Founded in 1969, along with the Loyola University Medical Center, the Health Sciences Campus in [Maywood](/wiki/Maywood,_Illinois "Maywood, Illinois") is the home of the [Stritch School of Medicine](/wiki/Stritch_School_of_Medicine "Stritch School of Medicine"), the Marcella Niehoff School of Nursing, and several programs that are part of the School of Continuing and Professional Studies. Maywood is a nearby western suburb of Chicago, approximately eleven miles from the Loop.

Loyola University Medical Center comprises the main hospital, Loyola Outpatient Center, Ronald McDonald Children's Hospital, Cardinal Bernardin Cancer Center, and several medical office and laboratory buildings. In 2011, the medical center was sold to [Trinity Health](/wiki/Trinity_Health_(Livonia,_Michigan) "Trinity Health (Livonia, Michigan)"), while Loyola continues to own and operate the academic buildings and select research facilities on campus.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

In 2017, Loyola expanded its accelerated nursing program to an online-based format with lab in [Downers Grove](/wiki/Downer%27s_Grove,_Illinois "Downer's Grove, Illinois").[[25]](#cite_note-25)

### John Felice Rome Center

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Loyola_University_Chicago&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: John Felice Rome Center")]

Loyola's permanent campus in Rome opened in 1962 at Casa Italiana Viaggi Internazionali Studenti (C.I.V.I.S.), a dormitory originally built to host athletes during the 1960 Summer Olympics. In 1978, the campus moved to its current location on [Monte Mario](/wiki/Monte_Mario "Monte Mario"), approximately two miles northwest of [Vatican City](/wiki/Vatican_City "Vatican City"). The campus is the home of the oldest American university program in Italy,[[26]](#cite_note-26) and hosts students from both Loyola and other universities seeking to study abroad. In 2005, the campus was renamed in honor of founder and Director Emeritus John P. Felice.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

### Additional campuses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Loyola_University_Chicago&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Additional campuses")]

In 2010, Loyola founded the Retreat and Ecology Campus on the former site of the Resurrection Retreat Center in [Woodstock](/wiki/Woodstock,_Illinois "Woodstock, Illinois"), Illinois, an outer suburb approximately fifty miles northwest of Chicago.[[28]](#cite_note-28) The campus houses the university's campus ministry programs, and offers a unique learning opportunity[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] for students and faculty interested in the sciences. The property contains 20 acres (8 ha) of natural habitat that includes ponds, streams, woods, and [prairie](/wiki/Prairie "Prairie").

Loyola also owns and operates the [Cuneo Mansion and Gardens](/wiki/Cuneo_Museum "Cuneo Museum") in suburban [Vernon Hills, Illinois](/wiki/Vernon_Hills,_Illinois "Vernon Hills, Illinois"), approximately thirty miles north of Chicago. The mansion and grounds were donated to the university in 2009, by the John and Herta Cuneo Foundation. The estate operates as a museum and hosts special events and a growing number of academic programs in business, education and law. Loyola has announced that it will cease operations there at the end of 2025 and is selling the site.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

### Tuition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Loyola_University_Chicago&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Tuition")]

For the 2022–2023 academic year, undergraduate tuition for new full-time students was $50,270 per year, not including room, board and fees, including the [CTA](/wiki/Chicago_Transit_Authority "Chicago Transit Authority") student transit 'U-Pass', Student Activity Fee, Technology Fee and mandatory health insurance.[[37]](#cite_note-37) Graduate school tuition varies, depending on the school.

### Rankings and demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Loyola_University_Chicago&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Rankings and demographics")]

Loyola University Chicago was ranked 115th among "National Universities" according to the 2019 *[U.S. News & World Report](/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report "U.S. News & World Report")* college rankings.

*[Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")* ranked Loyola University Chicago 211th out of the top 500 rated private and public colleges and universities in America for the 2024-25 report. Loyola was also ranked 113th among private colleges and 32nd in the Midwest[[38]](#cite_note-38)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Saint-iggy-statue.jpg/250px-Saint-iggy-statue.jpg)](/wiki/File:Saint-iggy-statue.jpg)

A statue of [Ignatius of Loyola](/wiki/Ignatius_of_Loyola "Ignatius of Loyola") was dedicated in 2000 and sits outside Cuneo Hall.

### Religious education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Loyola_University_Chicago&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Religious education")]

Loyola's Department of Theology offers undergraduate and graduate courses in the study of systematic theology, ethics, and Biblical studies, offering a diverse set of classes that are not limited to religious studies in a Catholic context.

Until 2025,[[39]](#cite_note-39) Loyola hosted a Jesuit First Studies Program, one of three in the country, with [Fordham University](/wiki/Fordham_University "Fordham University") and [Saint Louis University](/wiki/Saint_Louis_University_(United_States) "Saint Louis University (United States)") housing the other two. During a three-year period in the program, Jesuit Scholastics and Brothers generally studied philosophy and some theology. First Studies is one part of an eleven-year formation process toward the Jesuit priesthood. This program is administered by the Midwest Province of the [Society of Jesus](/wiki/Society_of_Jesus "Society of Jesus").

Until 2019, Loyola also hosted [Saint Joseph College Seminary](/wiki/Saint_Joseph_College_Seminary "Saint Joseph College Seminary"), which served the Catholic Archdiocese of Chicago and provided training to candidates for the diocesan priesthood.

Students at the John Felice Rome Center, [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"), [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy")

### Residential life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Loyola_University_Chicago&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Residential life")]

Most of the residence halls and apartments managed by Loyola's Department of Residence Life are in the Rogers Park and Edgewater neighborhoods, surrounding the Lake Shore Campus. One, Baumhart Hall, is at the Water Tower Campus on the Magnificent Mile in Chicago. Most of Loyola's residence halls are named after other Jesuit colleges and universities.

### Greek life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Loyola_University_Chicago&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Greek life")]

Loyola University Chicago is home to several Greek letter organizations. Among them are traditional social [fraternities and sororities](/wiki/Fraternities_and_sororities "Fraternities and sororities"), [professional co-ed fraternities](/wiki/Professional_fraternity "Professional fraternity"), and [cultural interest fraternities and sororities](/wiki/Cultural_interest_fraternities_and_sororities "Cultural interest fraternities and sororities").[[40]](#cite_note-40)

### College radio station

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Loyola_University_Chicago&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: College radio station")]

Loyola University owned and operated a low-power, carrier current radio station, WLUC-AM, in the 1960s. Students broadcast an eclectic music format on 600 kHz to Lake Shore Campus buildings and the surrounding Rogers Park neighborhood. WLUC-AM was replaced by an on-air FM station, [WLUW](/wiki/WLUW "WLUW"), in the 1970s.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

### Athletics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Loyola_University_Chicago&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Athletics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/LU_Wolf.jpg/250px-LU_Wolf.jpg)](/wiki/File:LU_Wolf.jpg)

LU Wolf at a Loyola Men's basketball game

Main article: [Loyola Ramblers](/wiki/Loyola_Ramblers "Loyola Ramblers")

NCAA Division I National Championships (3):
1963 Men's Basketball, 2014 Men's Volleyball, 2015 Men's Volleyball

Loyola is home to 11 varsity teams, most of which compete in NCAA Division I. The teams include men and women's basketball, cross country, men and women's golf, men and women's soccer, softball, track, and men and women's volleyball. The nickname "Ramblers" was first applied to Loyola's football team in 1926, because they frequently traveled throughout the United States.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

The LU Wolf is the mascot for the university. He was inspired by the [coat of arms](/wiki/Coat_of_arms "Coat of arms") of [St. Ignatius of Loyola](/wiki/Ignatius_of_Loyola "Ignatius of Loyola"), from whom Loyola derives its name, which depicts two wolves standing over a kettle. Taken from the heraldic crest carved in the lintel on St. Ignatius' family home in [Azpeitia](/wiki/Azpeitia "Azpeitia"), [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain"), the wolves and [cauldron](/wiki/Caldron_(heraldry) "Caldron (heraldry)") refer to the prosperity and generosity of the Loyola family, who, after feeding family, retainers and soldiers, had enough food to feed even the wild animals.

Loyola's men's basketball team, the Ramblers, won the 1963 national championship. Loyola is the only [Division I NCAA](/wiki/Division_I_(NCAA) "Division I (NCAA)") school in Illinois to have won a national title in men's basketball.[[43]](#cite_note-43) In 2018, they finished the NCAA tournament with the all-time highest win percentage of any team to have ever competed in the tournament.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

The [Loyola Ramblers men's volleyball](/wiki/Loyola_Ramblers_men%27s_volleyball "Loyola Ramblers men's volleyball") team won back-to-back NCAA championships in 2014[[45]](#cite_note-45) and 2015.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

In 2018, [Loyola's men's basketball](/wiki/2017%E2%80%9318_Loyola_Ramblers_men%27s_basketball_team "2017–18 Loyola Ramblers men's basketball team") team won the [Missouri Valley Conference](/wiki/Missouri_Valley_Conference "Missouri Valley Conference") tournament championship to qualify for the [2018 NCAA Division I men's basketball tournament](/wiki/2018_NCAA_Division_I_men%27s_basketball_tournament "2018 NCAA Division I men's basketball tournament"), for the first time since 1985.[[47]](#cite_note-lucMVC-47) The 11-seed Ramblers recorded four straight upset victories against the [Miami Hurricanes](/wiki/2017%E2%80%9318_Miami_Hurricanes_men%27s_basketball_team "2017–18 Miami Hurricanes men's basketball team"), [Tennessee Volunteers](/wiki/2017%E2%80%9318_Tennessee_Volunteers_basketball_team "2017–18 Tennessee Volunteers basketball team"), [Nevada Wolf Pack](/wiki/2017%E2%80%9318_Nevada_Wolf_Pack_men%27s_basketball_team "2017–18 Nevada Wolf Pack men's basketball team"), and [Kansas State Wildcats](/wiki/2017%E2%80%9318_Kansas_State_Wildcats_men%27s_basketball_team "2017–18 Kansas State Wildcats men's basketball team") to advance to the Final Four.[[48]](#cite_note-48) However, the [Michigan Wolverines](/wiki/2017%E2%80%9318_Michigan_Wolverines_men%27s_basketball_team "2017–18 Michigan Wolverines men's basketball team") defeated Loyola in the Final Four.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

In October 2018, the Loyola Ramblers Women's Soccer team won the [Missouri Valley Conference](/wiki/Missouri_Valley_Conference "Missouri Valley Conference") regular season title with 6–1 win over Evansville. The win gave the Ramblers their first Missouri Valley Conference regular season title in program history.[[50]](#cite_note-50)
